sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, MOUSE(row=19, col=48).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 72, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 71 actions; a strong score needs about 59 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

Current layout:

- Blue at [34,8]-[37,11] (4x4)

I've been going in circles trying different configurations. Let me reconsider the goal entirely.

These are "T" shapes. The left side has blocks that can form "T" shapes. But I've tried many configurations without success.

The red shape on the right is a "T" shape with:

The blue shape on the right is also a "T" shape with:

- A vertical bar extending downward from the center (rows 36-38, cols 48-50)
So both are "T" shapes with the horizontal bar at the top and the vertical bar extending downward.
On the left side, the blocks are:

- Red block: can be 6x4 (vertical) or 4x6 (horizontal)
- Gray block: 4x4 (fixed)
- Dark red block: 4x4 (fixed)
To form a "T" shape like the right side, I need:
- Horizontal bar at the top
- Vertical bar extending downward from the center
The gray block is at [30,8]-[33,11], which is at the left end of the horizontal bar. The dark red block is at [30,18]-[33,21], which is at the right end of the horizontal bar. The red block can be at [30,12]-[33,17], which is in the middle of the horizontal bar.
So the horizontal bar is: gray + red + dark red at rows 30-33, cols 8-21. This is 14 cols wide.
For a "T" shape, the vertical bar should extend downward from the center of the horizontal bar. The center of the horizontal bar is around cols 12-15 (the red block). So the vertical bar should be at cols 12-15, extending downward from rows 30-33.
But there's no block below the red block at rows 34-37, cols 12-15. The blue block is at [34,8]-[37,11], which is below the gray block, not the red block.
Maybe I need to move the blue block to be below the red block. But the blue block is 4x4, and the target slots I know are [20,18]-[23,21] and [34,8]-[37,11]. There's no slot at [34,12]-[37,15].
Wait, maybe I need to look at this differently. The right side has TWO separate "T" shapes: one red and one blue. The left side needs to form TWO separate "T" shapes as well.
Looking at the current layout:
- Horizontal bar: gray + red + dark red at rows 30-33
- Blue block at [34,8]-[37,11]
This forms one "T" shape with the horizontal bar at rows 30-33 and the blue block below the gray block. But this is not a proper "T" shape because the blue block is at the left end, not the center.
Maybe the goal is to have the blue block form a separate "T" shape. But the blue block is only 4x4, which is not enough to form a "T" shape.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal is not to form "T" shapes, but to match the colors and positions of the right side shapes.
The right side has:
- Red shape at rows 17-21, cols 42-54
- Blue shape at rows 34-38, cols 42-54
The left side has:

- Blue block at [34,8]-[37,11]
Maybe the goal is to have the red block at a position corresponding to the red shape on the right, and the blue block at a position corresponding to the blue shape on the right.
The red shape on the right is at rows 17-21. The red block on the left is at rows 30-33. These don't match.
The blue shape on the right is at rows 34-38. The blue block on the left is at rows 34-37. These match in terms of row range!
So maybe the goal is to have the blue block at rows 34-37, which it currently is. And the red block at rows 17-21, which it's not.
But the red block can only go to [24,18]-[29,21] or [30,12]-[33,17]. Neither is at rows 17-21.
Wait, maybe I need to look at the relative positions, not the absolute positions. The red shape on the right is above the blue shape. On the left, the red block should be above the blue block.
Currently, the red block is at rows 30-33 and the blue block is at rows 34-37. So the red block is above the blue block. This matches.

Looking at the ascii, this might be the gray block at the top. Let me check the ascii more carefully.
Actually, I see a gray block at [18,22]-[23,27] in the segmentation. This is a 6x4 block. But I thought the gray block was at [30,8]-[33,11]. Let me re-examine.

Let me look at the ascii more carefully to understand the layout.
Actually, I think I need to step back and reconsider the entire puzzle. The right side has two shapes: a red "T" shape and a blue "T" shape. The left side has blocks that can be arranged to form similar shapes.

But I don't know how to move blocks to arbitrary positions. The blocks can only go to specific target slots.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the green dot. It's at the bottom, in the grid at [38,8]-[43,13]. The green dot moves when I press UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT. And clicking on the right side shapes moves the corresponding colored block to the target slot indicated by the green dot.
So the green dot is the selector for the target slot. The yellow dot might be something else.
Let me look at the yellow dot more carefully. It's at the top, near the gray block. Maybe it's a target indicator for the gray block.
Actually, I just realized something. The gray block at [18,22]-[23,27] has a yellow dot inside it. This might be a target position for some block. But I don't know which block.
Let me try a different approach. Let me see if there's a configuration where all blocks form a specific pattern.

OK so I've tried all 4 combinations. None work. Let me think about what else could be the goal.

But now the blue block is still at [34,8]-[37,11], and the checkerboard block is gone. So the checkerboard block is not a separate entity; it's just the appearance of the slot when no block is there.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current green dot: (38-39, 12-13) = (top, right)
# I haven't tested (top, right) for the blue block. Let me try clicking on the blue shape.

result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 48}])
print(f"Clicked blue shape with green dot at (top, right). done={last_action_result.get('done')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nBlocks after click:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
    min_r, max_r = min(rows), max(rows)
    min_c, max_c = min(cols), max(cols)
    if max_c < 30 and n['pixels'] > 4:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, "
              f"bbox=[{min_r},{min_c}]-[{max_r},{max_c}]")

print(f"\nLevel done: {last_action_result.get('done', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

  color=R, pixels=24, bbox=[30,12]-[33,17]
  color=r, pixels=16, bbox=[30,18]-[33,21]
  color=g, pixels=32, bbox=[38,8]-[43,13]

for n in seg['nodes']:
    rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
    min_r, max_r = min(rows), max(rows)
    min_c, max_c = min(cols), max(cols)
    if max_c < 30 and n['pixels'] > 4:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, "
              f"bbox=[{min_r},{min_c}]-[{max_r},{max_c}]")
